# Git for Beginners: The Daily Loop

This chapter teaches the small set of commands you will use every
working day: see what changed, stage it, commit it, and look back at
your history. It also teaches the two skills that turn Git from
scary to safe: telling Git what to ignore, and undoing mistakes
without losing work.

Everything here is run for real inside the book's running example,
the minimum-wage difference-in-differences project in `sandbox/`. The
terminal blocks show the actual output those commands produced. Your
commit hashes will differ (they are content-based and unique to your
machine); nothing else should.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
This chapter stays entirely local. There is no GitHub yet, no remote,
no internet. You can do everything here on a laptop on a plane.
Pushing to GitHub is Chapter 9.
:::

## The mental model in one paragraph

Git tracks your project as a series of snapshots called **commits**.
A commit is a labelled save point for the whole project, not just one
file. Between commits, a changed file lives in one of three places:
the **working tree** (your actual files on disk), the **staging
area** or index (a holding pen for the next commit), and the
**repository** (the permanent history of commits). The daily loop is
just moving changes from the working tree, into the staging area,
into a commit. Chapter 3 draws this in full; you do not need the
diagram to start.

## The daily loop {.tier-beginner}

### Step 1: see where you are

Start every session by asking Git what it sees. `git status` is the
command you will run more than any other.

```bash
$ git status
On branch main
nothing to commit, working tree clean
```

A clean working tree means every change is already committed. This is
the state you want to return to before you stop for the day.

Now suppose you edit a script. Here we add a one-line `TODO` to the
cleaning script, reminding ourselves to drop tiny counties later.
`git status -s` gives the short view, and `git diff` shows exactly
what changed.

```bash
$ git status -s
 M scripts/01_clean.py

$ git diff
diff --git a/scripts/01_clean.py b/scripts/01_clean.py
index 1433ced..1455584 100644
--- a/scripts/01_clean.py
+++ b/scripts/01_clean.py
@@ -53,3 +53,5 @@ def main() -> None:

 if __name__ == "__main__":
     main()
+
+# TODO: drop counties with <100 teens
```

The `M` means modified. Lines starting with `+` are additions. You
have changed one file, and Git has noticed, but nothing is saved yet.

### Step 2: stage what you want to keep

Staging is how you choose what goes into the next commit. You can
stage one file at a time, which is the habit worth building.

```bash
$ git add scripts/01_clean.py
$ git status -s
M  scripts/01_clean.py
```

Look closely: the `M` moved from the right column to the left. In the
short status, the **left column is the staging area** and the right
column is the working tree. The change is now staged and ready.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
Beginners reflexively type `git add .` to stage everything. In a
research project that is how data files, output figures, and stray
`.env` secrets sneak into history. Stage files by name, or review
first with `git status` and `git diff --staged`. GitHub's own
guidance on avoiding leaks says the same thing: prefer `git add
filename` over the catch-all `git add .`.
:::

### Step 3: commit, with a message that will mean something later

A commit needs a message. Write it for the person who will read the
log in six months, which is usually you.

```bash
$ git commit -m "Note small-sample filter as a TODO"
[main e57cce6] Note small-sample filter as a TODO
 1 file changed, 2 insertions(+)
```

Git reports the branch (`main`), the new commit's short hash
(`e57cce6`), and a one-line summary of what changed. The snapshot is
now permanent history.

### Step 4: look back

`git log --oneline` is the compact history. The most recent commit is
on top.

```bash
$ git log --oneline
e57cce6 Note small-sample filter as a TODO
dcb18de Note headline result in README
f9a193f Add DiD analysis, report, and main table
758175e Add polars cleaning script
20c7835 Set up project skeleton and data generator
```

That is the whole loop: `status`, `diff`, `add`, `commit`, `log`.
Four of those five commands only look; only `commit` writes history.

::: {.callout-tip .recovery title="RECOVERY"}
Wrote a bad commit message? If you have not done anything since,
`git commit --amend -m "Better message"` rewrites just the last
commit. Do this only for commits you have not yet shared with anyone
(more on why in Chapter 7).
:::

## What to commit, and what to ignore {.tier-beginner}

A research repo contains things that must never be committed: raw
data, derived data, large outputs, and secrets. You tell Git to
ignore them with a `.gitignore` file. The running example ships with
one that ignores the data and output directories and any `.env` file.

Watch what happens when those files exist on disk. We create a fake
raw-data file and a `.env` holding a pretend API key.

```bash
$ ls data/raw/ .env
.env

data/raw/:
county_panel.csv

$ git status -s
```

`git status` shows nothing. Both paths match `.gitignore`, so Git
pretends they are not there and will never stage them. To confirm an
ignore rule is actually firing, ask for the ignored entries
explicitly:

```bash
$ git status --ignored -s
!! .env
!! data/
```

The `!!` marks ignored paths. This is the single most important habit
in research Git: your code is versioned, your data and secrets are
not. Chapters 6 and 8 go deep on why and on what to do with the data
instead.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
Ignoring data is not the same as losing it. The running example
rebuilds `data/raw/` from a seeded script (`00_make_data.py`), so a
collaborator who clones the code-only repo can regenerate the exact
same inputs. Code in Git plus a script that produces the data is
reproducible. Code plus a 2 GB CSV nobody can rebuild is not.
:::

## Undoing mistakes safely {.tier-beginner}

Most "I broke Git" panics are one of three situations. Each has a
safe, specific fix. None of them require deleting your repo and
re-cloning.

### Discard an edit you have not staged

You changed a file, you regret it, you have not staged or committed.
`git restore` puts the file back to its last committed state.

```bash
$ git status -s
 M README.md

$ git restore README.md
$ git status -s
```

Clean again. The experimental edit is gone.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
`git restore <file>` permanently throws away uncommitted changes to
that file. That is the point, but there is no undo for work that was
never committed. When in doubt, commit first (you can always undo a
commit), then experiment.
:::

### Unstage something you staged too early

You ran `git add`, then realised the change is not ready. You want to
keep the edit but take it out of the next commit. Add `--staged`.

```bash
$ git restore --staged README.md
$ git status -s
 M README.md
```

The file is still modified (right column), just no longer staged.
Nothing was lost; you only changed your mind about the next commit.

### Undo a change you already committed

This is the important one for shared work. You committed something
and want to reverse it. Do **not** rewrite history if anyone might
have your commits. Instead, `git revert` creates a *new* commit that
undoes the old one, leaving the record intact.

```bash
$ git revert --no-edit HEAD
[main 7b13816] Revert "Note small-sample filter as a TODO"
 Date: Sun Jun 21 21:48:27 2026 +0000
 1 file changed, 2 deletions(-)

$ git log --oneline -3
7b13816 Revert "Note small-sample filter as a TODO"
e57cce6 Note small-sample filter as a TODO
dcb18de Note headline result in README
```

Both commits are in the history: the original and its reversal. That
is honest and safe. Anyone who already had `e57cce6` just receives
the revert on top, with no conflict.

## The safety net you did not know you had {.tier-advanced}

Even genuinely destructive commands are usually recoverable, because
Git keeps a private log of everywhere `HEAD` has been: the
**reflog**. Suppose you wipe out two commits with a careless hard
reset.

```bash
$ git reset --hard HEAD~2
HEAD is now at dcb18de Note headline result in README
```

Those two commits look gone from `git log`. They are not gone. The
reflog still points at them.

```bash
$ git reflog -3
dcb18de HEAD@{0}: reset: moving to HEAD~2
7b13816 HEAD@{1}: revert: Revert "Note small-sample filter as a TODO"
e57cce6 HEAD@{2}: commit: Note small-sample filter as a TODO
```

`HEAD@{1}` is where you were before the reset. Point `HEAD` back at
it and the work returns.

```bash
$ git reset --hard 7b13816
HEAD is now at 7b13816 Revert "Note small-sample filter as a TODO"
```

::: {.callout-tip .recovery title="RECOVERY"}
If you ever think you have lost commits, run `git reflog` before
doing anything else, find the hash of the state you want, and
`git reset --hard <hash>` (or, more safely, `git switch -c rescue
<hash>` to inspect it on a new branch first). Reachable reflog
entries are kept for 90 days by default, and unreachable ones for 30,
so you have weeks, not seconds, to recover. The reflog is local only;
it is not a substitute for committing and, later, pushing.
:::

## What you can now do

You can run the full daily loop, keep data and secrets out of the
repository, and undo every common mistake without panic. That is
genuinely most of day-to-day Git. Branching, merging, and the sharper
power tools are Chapter 7; putting any of this online is Chapter 9.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

Working in your clone of the running example:

1. Open `report.qmd` and add a sentence to the discussion. Run
   `git status` and `git diff` to confirm Git sees exactly your
   change and nothing else.
2. Stage and commit it with a message a stranger could understand.
   Run `git log --oneline` and find your commit on top.
3. Make a second edit, stage it, then change your mind and
   `git restore --staged` it. Confirm with `git status -s` that the
   edit survives but is unstaged.
4. Create a file `data/raw/scratch.csv`. Run `git status`. Explain in
   one sentence why it does not appear, then prove it with
   `git status --ignored`.
5. Commit anything, then `git revert --no-edit HEAD`. Read the log
   and describe, in your own words, the difference between reverting a
   commit and deleting it.
